meta.title: A uniqueness theorem of Beurling for Fourier transform pairs

sections.body:
Let $f\in L^1(\mathbb{R})$ and assume that
\[\iint|f(x)||\hat{f}(y)|e^{|xy|}\,dx\,dy<\infty\]
where the Fourier transform $\hat{f}$ is defined by
\[\hat{f}(y)=\int e^{-ixy}f(x)\,dx.\]
Then $f=0$.

Set $M(y)=\int|f(x)|e^{|xy|}\,dx$, $\tilde{M}(x)=\int|\hat{f}(y)|e^{|xy|}\,dy$.

(1) $\int|\hat{f}(y)|\tilde{M}(y)\,dy=\int|f(x)|M(x)\,dx=\iint|f(x)||\hat{f}(y)|e^{|xy|}\,dx\,dy<\infty$.

If $f$ has compact support then $M$ grows exponentially unless $f=0$, forcing $f=0$ by analyticity. Same for $\hat{f}$. If neither has compact support, $M$ and $\tilde{M}$ grow faster than any exponential, so $f$ and $\hat{f}$ are entire.

(2) $F(z)=f(z)\hat{f}(iz)$. $F$ is entire and bounded on the real and imaginary axes. If $\lim M(x)e^{-cx^2/2}=0$ for all $c>0$, the Phragmén–Lindelöf theorem forces $F$ bounded, hence $f=0$. Otherwise $M(x)>e^{cx^2/2}$, $\tilde{M}(y)>e^{\tilde{c}y^2/2}$ for large $x,y$, giving $|f(z)|\leq Ke^{|\Im z|^2/4c}$, so $f$ is of order $2$ or $0$.

(4) $\int_0^\infty|f(re^{i\alpha/2})|^2\,dr\leq K'$ for $0<\alpha<\pi/2$. Letting $\alpha\to\pi/2$ gives $\int_0^\infty|f(re^{i\pi/4})|^2\,dr\leq K'$. Same for $\hat{f}$, hence $|F(re^{i\pi/4})|$ is bounded; Phragmén–Lindelöf gives $F$ constant, so $f(z)\hat{f}(iz)=0$, implying $f=0$.

If $\varphi$ and $\psi$ are conjugate convex functions then
\[\int|f(x)|e^{\varphi(x)}\,dx<\infty,\quad\int|\hat{f}(y)|e^{\psi(y)}\,dy<\infty\implies f=0.\]

For $1<p<\infty$, let $Z_p$ be the space of entire functions $\varphi$ such that for some $A,B,C$,
\[|\varphi(z)|\leq Ce^{A|\Im z|^{p'}-B|\Re z|^p},\quad z\in\mathbb{C},\]
where $1/p+1/p'=1$. The Fourier transform maps $Z_p$ onto $Z_{p'}$.

If $\varphi\in Z_p$, then $|\varphi(x)|\leq Ce^{-B|x|^p}$, $|\hat{\varphi}(\xi)|\leq C'e^{-B'|\xi|^{p'}}$.

If $\varphi\in Z_p$ with $|\varphi(x)|\leq Ce^{-B|x|^p}$ and $|\hat{\varphi}(\xi)|\leq C'e^{-B'|\xi|^{p'}}$, then Beurling's theorem implies $(Bp)^{1/p}(B'p')^{1/p'}\geq 1$ whenever $\varphi\not\equiv 0$.

$Z_p$ is non-trivial when $2\leq p<\infty$. By Fourier duality, $Z_p$ is non-trivial for $1<p\leq 2$ as well. For $2\leq p\leq 4$, an even subharmonic function $\varphi_0$ with $\varphi_0(z)=-\Re z^p$ ($\Re z\geq 0$) yields analytic functions in $Z_p$. This extends to all $p\geq 2$ by considering $z\mapsto\varphi(z^k)$.
